behavior_in_task_description.reason: Tests assert exact literal strings like 'progress.OneOff(ctx, "resolve "+p.src.Reference.String())' and 'progress.OneOff(ld.pctx, fmt.Sprintf("pulling %s", ld.desc.Digest))' including specific variable names (ld.pctx, p.src.Reference) that are not mentioned in instruction.md. The instruction says to use 'the same context variable and progress ID string' but never specifies the exact variable names or exact call-site strings. Also, the test for writer.go asserts the progress import must be remove

no_solution_leakage.reason: The instruction explicitly says 'Replace each call to the local helper function with the appropriate progress.OneOff call' — this names the exact replacement function (progress.OneOff) and tells the agent precisely what to do. It also says 'Remove the local helper function definitions' and 'The buildkit progress package provides a OneOff function with the same signature as the local helpers.' This is stating the fix, not just describing what's broken.

solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: Tests assert exact literal strings like 'progress.OneOff(ctx, "resolve "+p.src.Reference.String())' with no tolerance for formatting variations (e.g., extra spaces, variable renaming of the done callback). The test test_correct_number_of_call_sites counts exact occurrences of 'progress.OneOff(' expecting exactly 5. test_writer_go_no_local_oneoffprogress checks that the progress import is absent. These are highly specific to the gold solution's exact implementation.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: The fail-to-pass tests (test_pull_go_uses_progress_oneoff, test_export_go_uses_progress_oneoff, test_worker_go_uses_progress_oneoff, test_writer_go_no_local_oneoffprogress, test_no_duplicate_oneoffprogress_anywhere, test_correct_number_of_call_sites) are ALL purely text-matching assertions using 'assert X in content' or 'content.count()'. While there are also compilation/vet/test tests, the f2p tests are entirely string-based.

test_not_tautological.reason: The fail-to-pass tests check for specific code transformations (presence of progress.OneOff calls, absence of local oneOffProgress definitions) that cannot be satisfied by a stub or no-op implementation — the original code has oneOffProgress defined locally, so the tests would fail without actual changes.

anti_cheating_measures.reason: The solution is in /workspace/moby's git history or could be found in solution/solve.sh. The agent could read the test file at tests/test_outputs.py to see the exact literal strings expected and simply produce those strings. The exact expected content strings are in the test assertions.

behavior_in_tests.reason: The instruction requires: compilation succeeds, go vet passes, unit tests pass, no duplicate helper definitions remain. All of these are tested.

informative_test_structure.reason: Tests are well-named with descriptive function names and docstrings indicating pass-to-pass vs fail-to-pass, grouped by file and concern.

structured_data_schema.reason: This task involves code refactoring, not structured data output.

no_typos.reason: File paths, package names, function names, and commands in instruction.md appear correct and consistent with the codebase.

instruction_no_hint_leakage.reason: The instruction names the exact replacement function 'progress.OneOff' and tells the agent to use 'go doc github.com/moby/buildkit/util/progress' to find it. It also names all four specific files and tells the agent exactly what to replace (local helper with progress.OneOff). This is significant hint leakage for a refactoring task.

config_edit_scope_bounded.reason: This is a code-only refactoring task, not a config edit task.
